title: Perpetuity: The Yotsuba Group (ヨツバグループ) pays a 320 perpetuity at the...
summary: Calculate the effective annual rate or convert between APR and EAR under different compounding assumptions.
prompt:
The Yotsuba Group (ヨツバグループ) pays a $320 perpetuity at the end of every 3
years starting the end of this year. In other words, the first payment is at the end
of year 1, the second payment at the end of year 4, and so on. Note that the
discount rate is 7.8860% compounded 12 time(s) per year. What is the present
value of the Yotsuba perpetuity?
solution:
Step 1 Highlight Relevant Information
The Yotsuba Group (ヨツバグループ) pays a $320 perpetuity at the end of every 3
years starting the end of this year. In other words, the first payment is at the end
of year 1, the second payment at the end of year 4, and so on. Note that the
discount rate is 7.8860% compounded 12 time(s) per year. What is the present
value of the Yotsuba perpetuity?
A few things to note:
The first highlighted bit suggests that we're dealing with a perpetuity (i.e., P
PMT/k); therefore, there's only one formula to use
The second highlighted bit suggests that our perpetuity's "PMT" occurs every 3
years in perpetuity rather than every year
The third highlighted bit indicates when "PMTs" are occuring on our timeline
The fourth highlighted bit (i.e., 7.8860% represents "QR"; therefore, this figure
must be compounded 12 time(s) per year to earn the Effective Annual Rate EAR
The fifth highlighted bit informs us that our mission is to solve for the present
value of this perpetuity
Step 2 Define Key & Missing Variables
The formula for Perpetuities can be written as follows
PVn=PMTn+1kPV_{n}=\frac{PMT_{n+1}}{k}PVn=kPMTn+1
PMT14 2
K14 EPR
PVn: ?
The formula to set Quoted Rates to Effective Annual Rates can be written as
follows
EAR1QRm)m−1EAR \left 1\frac{QR}{m} \right )^{m}-1EAR1+mQR)m−1
QR 7.8860%
Calculator studio solutions 8
m: 12
EAR ?
The formula to set Effective Annual Rates to Effective Periodic Rates can be
writtten as follows
EPR1EAR1/m−1EPR \left 1EAR \right )^{1/m}-1EPR1EAR1/m−1
EAR ?
m:  1/3
EPR ?
Step 3 Build our Timeline
Note that our timeline seems to display a recurring pattern of cash flows
happening every 3 years. Therefore, it would be best practice to consider this
individual $320 perpetuity PMT as one spanning 3 years.
Such that we can write PMT14 $320.00
Step 5 Solving for the Effective Periodic Rate
Our "PMT" spans 3 years instead of one; therefore, it wouldn't make sense to set
"K" as being equal to 8.1774%. In this case, our "EAR" will have to be set to an
Effective Periodic Rate EPR that will reflect the entire compounding effect earned
over 3 years.
The same concepts we've applied to converting our "QR" to an "EAR" will hold
here as well. Therefore, 8.1774% would not represent the entire that was
generated, and neither would simply multiplying this number by 3!
The formula to set Effective Annual Rates to Effective Periodic Rates can be
writtten as follows
EPR1EAR1/m−1EPR \left 1EAR \right )^{1/m}-1EPR1EAR1/m−1
EAR 8.1774%
m:  1/3
Such that EPR 26.5930%
Step 6 Solving our Perpetuity
The formula for Perpetuities can be written as follows
Calculator studio solutions 9
PVn=PMTn+1kPV_{n}=\frac{PMT_{n+1}}{k}PVn=kPMTn+1
PMT14 $320.00
K14 26.5930%
Such that, PV2 $1,203.33
Timeline
A few things to note:
Let us not forget an essential rule to perpetuities and their PV. The PV of a
perpetuity PMT occuring every year will always be one period before the initial
perpetuity PMT. For example, PV will be at t=2 if PMT was at t=3, and PV will be at
t=0 if PMT was at t=1
However, what about grouped perpetuity PMTs? In other words, PMTs that span
several years. In these cases, it is important to remember that the PV of a
perpetuity PMT spanning several years will always be several periods before the
initial grouped perpetuity PMT. For example, PV will be at t=3 if PMT spans 5
years and it started at t=8 (i.e., 8 5 3, and PV will be at t=-2 if PMT spans 3
years and it started at t=1 (i.e., 1 3 2
In the Yotsuba perpetuity problem, the intial PMT occurs at t=1 and it spans a
period of 3 years; therefore, our perpetuity formula will return PV at t=-2
However, this isn't our present value today (i.e., PV0 so we need to compound
our PV for 2 periods to get PV at t=0
Timeline
PV at t=0 can be computed by using the formula below
FVPV1K)nFVPV1K^{n}FVPV1K)n
Such that, PV0 $1,408.17
